title: SEGMENT 14 — The Human Pangenome Reference Consortium

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The Human Pangenome Reference Consortium
T2T-CHM13 is a complete genome, but it is still one genome — and as we said in Segment 12, no single genome can represent human variation.
The next frontier, and this is where the field is actively living right now in 2026, is the Human Pangenome.
The Human Pangenome Reference Consortium, HPRC, was launched in 2019 with funding from NHGRI, and published its first draft pangenome in Nature in May 2023.
The idea of a pangenome is to replace the single linear reference sequence with a graph.

Instead of one string of three billion bases, you have a graph in which each chromosome is represented as a network of nodes — shared sequence that everybody has — and branches — alternate sequences carried by different individuals or populations.
The 2023 draft pangenome contains forty-seven assemblies, each from a different individual, each sequenced and assembled to near-T2T quality using long reads.
Together, those forty-seven assemblies represent ninety-four haplotypes — two per individual — drawn from diverse populations around the world.

The pangenome graph captures variation that simply cannot exist in a single linear reference: regions where some people carry sequence that other people lack entirely, structural rearrangements that differ between haplotypes, large insertions that are present in some populations and absent in others.
Here is the analogy.
The 2003 linear reference is a single recipe for chocolate cake — flour, sugar, eggs, butter, cocoa, in specific amounts and a specific order.
The pangenome is a cookbook of forty-seven variant cake recipes from forty-seven bakers around the world, with the shared steps consolidated and the divergent steps shown as alternative paths.
Some bakers add cinnamon.
Some substitute coconut flour.
Some swap milk chocolate for dark.
The pangenome lets you see, at every point in the recipe, what the actual range of human variation looks like, rather than treating one baker's recipe as canonical and everyone else's as deviation.

The HPRC's stated goal is to scale up to three hundred and fifty individual assemblies by the late 2020s, with deliberate inclusion of historically underrepresented populations — African, Indigenous American, South Asian, Oceanic.

This matters scientifically because the original 2003 reference and most subsequent variation databases have been skewed toward European-ancestry samples, which means that clinical variant interpretation has been more accurate for people of European descent than for everybody else.
The pangenome is the future of the human reference.

It is harder to work with than a linear reference — graph-based alignment tools are still maturing, and most existing bioinformatics pipelines assume a linear coordinate system — but the field is steadily building the infrastructure to make graph references the default.
